text: Caffeine supplementation acutely enhances muscular endurance, movement velocity, muscular strength, sprinting, jumping, throwing performance, and a wide range of aerobic and anaerobic sport-specific actions, with small to moderate benefits.
Aerobic endurance is the form of exercise with the most consistent moderate-to-large benefits from caffeine use, although the magnitude differs between individuals.
Caffeine consistently improves exercise performance when consumed in doses of 3-6 mg/kg body mass, with minimal effective doses possibly as low as 2 mg/kg body mass.
Very high caffeine doses (e.g. 9 mg/kg body mass) are associated with a high incidence of side effects and do not provide additional ergogenic benefit over moderate doses.
The most commonly used timing for caffeine supplementation is 60 minutes pre-exercise, though caffeinated chewing gums may require a shorter waiting time than capsules.
Caffeine improves physical performance in both trained and untrained individuals.
Caffeine is ergogenic for endurance exercise in the heat (3-6 mg/kg) and at altitude (4-6 mg/kg).
Inter-individual differences in caffeine response, including adverse effects on sleep and anxiety, may be attributed to genetic variation in caffeine metabolism and habitual caffeine intake.
